item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait a minute? Doesn't the earth rotate at a fast speed and make this technically constantly move faster than 6m/s? What if the random alternate universe had some dangerous chemicals on the other side? Heck, what if the alternate universe was composed entirely composed of antimatter or the laws of physics were inverted?

I will leave this at a no-vote because of the enjoyable read

EDIT: Well, I guess I should've read the footnotes, and the chance of a pure antimatter universe is really small. I still think it's stupid for the foundation to allow exploration, even if there is just a small chance of reality destruction. I will change my no-vote to a tentative upvote.
EDIT 2: Should've read the article better I genuinely enjoy it now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this entry, and keep sending it to friends as part of the introduction to SCP, but I always have to include the proviso "[Important: click "Show exploration log" before reading the bit underneath]" - I wonder if that section could be uncollapsed by default? They are so crucial for getting the full impact of the writing…

Either way, thanks again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hah! I love this! Our Foundation being busted by the Dimension Police. +8

Though I would like a perspective of the D-Cops wondering what's happening to our space-time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So THAT'S what happens when you let all the Dust escape…

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded:
The question is, whether it's a cool idea that arises multiple times independently, or whether it's a sneaky way of trying to add a tribute to a particular (extremely good, and with a LOT of SCPfuel potential) young adult novel…

Also maybe a bit of what JD Reisz did after Unicorn Jelly, at least the parts which explored interesting UJ-like multiverses-with-different-physical-laws themes rather than the uncomfortable sexual fetishism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus. They are killing whole universes by wounding and infecting them. How do they not see this?

Wow, at +300.

And here I remember being happy if it never reached more than +25…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not just for breaking… also inadvertently destroying them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a bit extensive, but worth reading. The ending is spectacular, enjoyed it very much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is brilliant, and I'm dissapointed I haven't found this until now. Wish I could vote more than once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just noting that I've updated this SCP to be in-line with the new link to SCP-1165.

Otherwise, we might run into some quasi-headcanon issues where this thing was tested in 2011 yet 1165 was discovered and tested in 19██…

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I can see that link working, but honestly there's just not enough information regarding the multiverse's makup present in this SCP to really justify it.

I could write a tale from Sigma-Xotoxin's perspective to create some worldbuilding regarding 2207 and serve as a bridge between this SCP and that tale; I'm thinking the operant phrase would be along the lines of…

I swear, if the Gardeners don't do something about that branch, one of those 'verses is probably gonna accidentally prune the entire thing, and guess who has to deal with the paperwork!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I don't know. As good as the tale is, I think that sort of crosslink is a big stretch. If dankaar is fine with it I have no objections, but it's pretty tenuous.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
or if there was a sudden, inexplicable and hostile change in the alternate universe

TBH, I headcanoned 2207 as one of several reasons Day Broke.

Anyways, I have a further suggestion for cross linking, as we have a Tale which features a protagonist hopping across alt dimensions, with the implication that something the SCPF did caused the multiverse to merge:

IT IS BELIEVED THAT THE ACTIONS PERFORMED BY YOUR ORGANIZATION ARE BOTH WITHOUT MALICE OR FULL KNOWLEDGE OF THE RESULTING REPERCUSSIONS.

To:

IT IS BELIEVED THAT THE ACTIONS PERFORMED BY YOUR ORGANIZATION ARE BOTH WITHOUT MALICE OR FULL KNOWLEDGE OF THE RESULTING REPERCUSSIONS.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: It shall be done.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Author here.

This SCP also tends to play havoc on the natural laws that govern connected universes, to the point where they're pretty much inimical to life as we know it.

That said, a crosslink to another alternate-reality SCP can work if it's implied that the alternate reality was accessed/discovered after a test involving 2207 (or if there was a sudden, inexplicable and hostile change in the alternate universe that implies 2207 interference).

Test dates could be a problem (as 1165 was discovered in 19██), though simply changing the year on the exploration logs for this SCP would fix it. The date for this SCP can only be pushed back to about 1950 or so, as that's when plastic utensils started to become commonplace.

I'd suggest the Wikiwalk-suggested change be edited to read something along the lines of:

It has been later determined that at least three other alternate realities accessed by different SCPs, including SCP-1165, may be the result of prior testing with SCP-2207.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as shown below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Neat. Any way, its implied in the article that the Foundation has done some pretty extensive testing of SCP-2207, and explorations of the alternate universes it opens up to. It wouldn't be too far out of line if, during this testing, they opened a hole to an alternate universe that is accessed through another SCP object, like SCP-1165. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

The following is a partial, abridged exploration log. See Document 2207-24 for a full log of tests, explorations, and recovered material from SCP-2207-1 instances. In all tests, a mechanical arm was used to create SCP-2207-1 instances, and the SCP-2207-1 instance was propped open by a set of metal braces.

To this:

The following is a partial, abridged exploration log. See Document 2207-24 for a full log of tests, explorations, and recovered material from SCP-2207-1 instances. In all tests, a mechanical arm was used to create SCP-2207-1 instances, and the SCP-2207-1 instance was propped open by a set of metal braces. On no less than three occasions during testing, SCP-2207-1 instances have accessed alternate universes accessible through other SCP objects, including SCP-1165.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

A GREAT read, in my opinion. My only, obviously nitpicky, gripe was the use of the word "occulting/occulted" in test 25. The act of a solar eclipse is occlusion. I hope you don't mind that I edited that in, since I know that's what you meant.

+200!

Damn. I guess people really like their knives what cut dimensions!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted.

Man. That was one of the better SCPs I've read in a long time. Congrats on doing so well on your first SCP (I say, 2 years later). I really enjoyed the hints to what was really going on. Most of what I love about the way SCPs are written is that the clinical tone doesn't really allow for any overdramatic revelations or melodrama (in most cases), letting you slowly figure out the interesting—or just downright chilling—backstory by yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noticed those similarities too. Knife that cuts open portals to other universes, the creation of which actually fucks up the multiverse at large.

Of course the detrimental effect is MUCH worse in this article than in His Dark Materials, and the exploration logs + the C&D letter really set this one apart.

+1, very well executed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: other than some blatant similarities to the knife in the book The Subtle Knife, I still like where you went with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good. Excellently executed. The part about the melting universe had a unique eldritch vibe to it that made be a bit… uneasy. In the "watching a horror uneasy" way. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted without even looking at the experiment logs. Just the idea of an interdimensional regulatory body that sent the Foundation a C&D letter for breaking into universes was excellent.

(Once you open the logs, it becomes much less funny… but still good.)

item.title: Impressive
item.content:encoded: I am nearly speechless at this. Not because it's stunningly amazing, but because you ripped something directly from The Big List Of Overdone SCP Cliches and made it work. You made it work quite well. Definite upvote. I especially enjoyed the reference to Norse Mythology.

Woo!

*does the Happy Author Dance*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh boy. I remember reading this draft a reeeeeeally long time ago. While the concept of multiversal exploration has been written about a countless number of times, I really enjoyed the way it was portrayed here. This has been significantly upgraded from the original version that I read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes yes YES! Love it so much.

Tiny grammar note. I think this line:

[…] IT IS BELIEVED THAT THE ACTIONS PERFORMED BY YOUR ORGANIZATION ARE BOTH WITHOUT MALICE OR FULL KNOWLEDGE OF THE RESULTING REPERCUSSIONS.

might flow a bit better if it was phrased like this:

[…] IT IS BELIEVED THAT YOUR ORGANIZATION ACTED WITH NEITHER MALICE NOR FULL KNOWLEDGE OF THE RESULTING REPERCUSSIONS.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this scip, the knife may be the object but it isn't the focus in a believable way. So yeah…

a really clever OTT I guess +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is cute, but although I was amused it didn't really do anything much else for me. I felt like the extradimensional authority aspect, while amusing, was kind of the obvious route for this thing to take. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooooh. 093 meets The Subtle Knife. I really like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP, which looks like a mundane object but spawns extradimensional multiverse-breaking effects, with well done exploration logs, follows in the footsteps of 093 and 1165, among others.

Upvoted, with the force of a thousand stars.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When Yggdrasil was mentioned, I immediately thought of The Infinite Loops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read about a knife that cuts dimensions and was all ready to downvote, then the logs came… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sort of like Marvel's Time Variance Authority, which I was reminded of when I read this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who do you think Blackboxed Military Service is? :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When it came down to the Office of Multiversal Incidents, I was half-expecting them to threaten us by sending Jet Li.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me a great deal of 093.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And that one someone else wrote (and failed with) recently, that annoyed you so. :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe you're thinking of Number 11 from The Big List of Overdone SCP Cliches?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those exploration logs are fantastic, but I wish they were attached to a more interesting/original object. I know I've seen a knife that cuts through time and space somewhere on this wiki, either as another article, or as an example of something or other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good thing they stopped too. God knows what would happen if they opened a rift only to look at themselves opening a rift on the other side.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2207 Project Lead: Here's a nifty thing. Let's see what it can do.

Interuniversal Police Agency: Stop that.

2207 Project Lead: You know, maybe we should definitely never, ever do that again.

I like the image of a dispassionate extradimensional bureaucrat skimming the form letter and muttering about how that's the third one this week as he signs it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Things like this, with exploration logs containing horrifying stuff, always feel a lot like 093.

But heck, that's not a bad thing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tautologies aren't necessarily bad, when they help confirm something. There are a lot of skips that resemble mundane objects, but with unusual features, modifications, and/or markings in addition to their anomalous properties. "Outside of its anomalous properties, it is not anomalous" confirms that this is not such an item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … I never wrote that one. That was a completely different SCP that I was slightly miffed at seeing, since I was actively writing this one at the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It doesn't have any odd markings, it isn't remarkably cold or hot, it looks, feels, and smells just like any other disposable plastic knife. It just has this odd property of tearing rents in spacetime…

I remember the original version of this, back when it was a butter knife with far too many links to SCP-106. I like this one a lot better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Little bit. But I wanted to make it absolutely clear that it's nothing special outside of the fact that it creates portals. You sometimes see SCPs whose physical qualities don't match non-anomalous analogs, even when the actual anomaly is something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fun.

One thing:

Testing has indicated that, outside of SCP-2207's anomalous properties, it is identical to other non-anomalous disposable plastic knives.

Is this not a bit of a tautology? "Outside of its anomalous properties, it is not anomalous."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found it amusing when I read the draft and I continue doing so. It is interesting and it is a nice take on a very OTT concept.

However what took my upvote was the letter. When I read it I can't stop imagining the lawful-stupid guys of the Hitchhiker Guide to the Galaxy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this when I read the draft, and I like the changes you've done to it. My only gripe would be that the test with the farm (which you must have added after I reviewed) makes the twist, that the Foundation is causing all of these apocalypses, a bit more obvious. Of course, having read the draft, I already knew that was the twist, so I'm not the best judge of how obvious the conclusion is made.

Still, though. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A decent elaboration of the "knife that cuts through space" concept, and the ending feels interesting too. I'm getting myself behind this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first SCP is here!

I'd like to thank everyone who took a peek at this draft, both in the forums and chat, you guys really helped me flesh this thing out.

Spoilers

What's going on?
I know what's going on!

The term "Yggdrasil-Severence event" is the major clue here. For those not in the know, "Yggdrasil" was the name of the World Tree in Norse mythology, which held all the various realms together. The term is, essentially, exactly what it says on the tin.
The knife here, in the process of creating a rift to another universe, severs the bonds that connect that universe to the rest of the Multiverse. Without these bonds, the laws of reality in those universes start to really go wild, and will almost always end up inhospitable to life as we know it, if not outright destroy that universe.
Since the laws of reality get screwed up, so does anything in there, whether or not they're native to that universe. Hence the Bad Stuff that happened to the D-Class and the drones.
… And yes, that letter is indeed a standard form, since this sort of things happens an infinite amount of times in an infinite multiverse. Just easier to print out a standard form and fill in the blanks than to write up a new one each time it occurs.

+The Hallway
- The Hallway

If you already read the above, then you don't need me to tell you that the D-class isn't the only one suffering from "can't reach my destination!" syndrome. What's going on there is that Zeno's Paradox of the Tortoise and Achilles has more-or-less become a Real Thing. Anyone/anything trying to get to anywhere must reach the halfway point. And to get there, they have to reach the halfway point of that, and so on, and so on. The end effect is that they can never reach their destination, no matter how fast or how much they move.

+The Eclipse
-The Eclipse

That's Mercury, and gravity from other worlds have gotten a LOT stronger.
Also, a Level C PPE is basically a "moonsuit", but takes in air from the outside and runs it through a powered air filtration system. Of course people will be staring at that D-Class!

+The Farm
-The Farm

Life is no longer possible in that universe, but that universe only realizes it when the wind carries the news.

+Frozen
-Frozen

Another one of Zeno's Paradoxes have largely become a Real Thing. In this case, the Paradox of the Arrow. In this instance, in any (duration-less) instance of time, no movement occurs as an object doesn't move from where it "is" to where it "isn't". Therefore, even if you stitch all those instances of time together, movement isn't possible, since no movement occurs in any given instance.
Note that this doesn't apply to, say, thinking. Which the D-Class (and everyone else in that universe) are more than capable of doing in their frozen state. And yes, there's a brief delay before that law applies to anything coming into that universe.
Finally, the Foundation quit using D-class because they were losing them at a rate of one D-class for every two tests. Drones are probably cheaper at that point.

+Melting
-Melting

Bad news: It takes a lot less energy (heat) to transform a solid to a liquid.
Good news: People can live as puddles! Wait, wait, that's still not good news… The larger the object is, however, the faster it melts.

+[No Signal]
-[No Signal]

Those [No Signal] patches are places where that universe doesn't exist anymore. It reads as [No Signal] because that's what happens if the monitors the Foundation uses aren't receiving data. Anything that touches those [No Signal] patches basically gets erased from existence.
